heroOverview: Iniencephaly is a rare and uniformly lethal neural tube defect characterised by a triad of occipital bone defect at the skull base, fixed retroflexion of the head onto the back (the spine bends backward to a severe degree), and an open cervical or cervicothoracic spine, often with accompanying brain and spinal cord malformations. The name derives from the Greek 'inion' (occiput). Affected fetuses typically display an extremely short neck or no visible neck at all on prenatal ultrasound because the head is fused or angled directly against the dorsal trunk. It is estimated to affect fewer than 1 per 100,000 pregnancies. Like all neural tube defects, it is strongly associated with periconceptional folate deficiency, and folic acid supplementation is the primary prevention strategy.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated fewer than 1 per 100,000 pregnancies; exact prevalence is uncertain due to rarity
keyStats.demographics: Female fetuses predominate at a ratio of approximately 2:1 to 4:1 over male fetuses; affects all ethnicities
keyStats.avgAge: Defect arises by day 24–26 of embryonic development; detectable prenatally from the first trimester
keyStats.globalCases: Fewer than several hundred cases are reported in the world literature; true global incidence is poorly characterised

definition: Iniencephaly (ICD-10: Q00.2) is a severe congenital central nervous system malformation comprising three core elements: a defect of the occipital bone at the skull base (foramen magnum region), extreme retroflexion of the cervical or cervicothoracic spine so that the occiput approaches or touches the posterior thorax, and an open spinal dysraphism in the affected segments. In some cases the defect is limited to the occiput and upper cervical spine (iniencephaly clausus, in which the skin covers the dysraphic spine), while in others the spinal cord is exposed (iniencephaly apertus). Brain anomalies are present in the majority of cases and include anencephaly, encephalocele, holoprosencephaly, hydrocephalus, and Dandy-Walker malformation. Cervical and thoracic vertebrae are frequently fused, malformed, or absent. The condition belongs to the spectrum of neural tube defects sharing the same primary risk factor of periconceptional folate deficiency. Specialist involvement spans clinical genetics, maternal-fetal medicine, and neonatology. No liveborn infant has ever survived iniencephaly with meaningful neurological function; the condition is considered universally lethal.

typesClassification.description: The open form, in which the dysraphic spinal defect is not covered by skin; the spinal cord is exposed to amniotic fluid, elevating AFP more dramatically. This is the more common form in most case series.

The closed form, in which overlying skin covers the spinal defect; the neural tube anomaly is structurally contained, though the retroflexion and occipital bone defect persist. AFP elevation may be less marked.

A severe combined form in which the defining features of iniencephaly coexist with absent cerebral hemispheres; thought to represent a related but distinct neural tube failure overlapping with craniorachischisis.

primarySymptoms: Extreme retroflexion of the fetal head on ultrasound, with the occiput appearing to touch or approach the dorsal thorax, creating an appearance of an absent or fused neck.
Absent or very short fetal neck visible on longitudinal ultrasound views, due to fixed backward angulation of the cervical spine.
Occipital bone defect identified as an irregular or absent occiput on fetal anatomy survey.
Open cervical or cervicothoracic spinal dysraphism — absent posterior vertebral arches with exposed neural tissue in the apertus form.
Polyhydramnios in many cases, caused by impaired fetal swallowing due to the rigid retroflexed posture and often co-existing brain anomalies.
Associated brain malformations visible on ultrasound, including encephalocele, hydrocephalus, and absent cerebral hemispheres in combined forms.
Fixed abnormal fetal posture — the fetus cannot flex the head forward normally and lies in a characteristic arched position.
Elevated maternal serum AFP in the apertus form from exposed neural tissue; may be less markedly elevated in the clausus form.
Vertebral anomalies — fused, missing, or malformed cervical and thoracic vertebrae identified on detailed spinal survey.
Kyphoscoliosis or scoliosis of the thoracic spine is common in surviving fetuses at the time of detailed anatomy survey.
earlyWarningSigns: Absent or very short fetal neck on first-trimester nuchal translucency scan at 11–14 weeks.
Fixed hyperextension of the fetal head in the absence of cord entanglement or external compression.
Elevated maternal serum AFP on second-trimester screening, particularly in the apertus form.
Failure to visualise a normal posterior cervical spine or occiput on first-trimester or early second-trimester scan.
emergencySigns: Obstructed labour due to the fixed hyperextended fetal head and rigid spine, requiring urgent obstetric assessment.
Severe polyhydramnios causing maternal respiratory distress, preterm labour, or uterine rupture risk.
Uterine overdistension from polyhydramnios requiring emergency amnioreduction.
Fetal compromise during labour in the presence of abnormal fetal presentation.

causes.description: Low folate intake around the time of neural tube closure impairs one-carbon metabolism essential for DNA replication in the rapidly dividing neuroepithelium. This is the primary modifiable cause of all open neural tube defects including iniencephaly.

causes.cause: Failure of cephalic neural tube closure combined with occipital bone developmental defect
causes.description: Current models suggest iniencephaly results from failure of the posterior neural tube to close properly at the occiput and upper cervical level, combined with a primary defect in occipital bone formation. The resulting unclosed neural tissue causes the spine to retroreflex to fill the defect.

Prenatal valproate exposure raises overall NTD risk 10–20-fold; the specific contribution to iniencephaly follows the same mechanism as for other NTDs — inhibition of folate-dependent histone deacetylases and disruption of early neuroepithelial development.

Hyperglycaemia during weeks 3–8 of gestation produces reactive oxygen species that disrupt neural fold elevation and apposition, increasing risk of all NTD subtypes including iniencephaly by 2–3-fold.
Trisomy 18 and trisomy 13 are found in a minority of iniencephaly cases, as with other severe NTDs. Chromosomal microarray is offered to all families.

causes.cause: Genetic variants in neural tube closure pathways
causes.description: Variants in folate-pathway genes (MTHFR 677C>T) and in genes regulating neural fold convergent extension (VANGL1, DACT1) have been identified in small case series of iniencephaly, but the specific genetic architecture is less well characterised than for craniorachischisis.

riskFactors.description: The principal modifiable risk factor for iniencephaly, as for all NTDs. Supplementation with 400 mcg daily before conception reduces risk by up to 70%; women with a prior NTD should take 4–5 mg daily.
Recurrence risk for any NTD after one affected pregnancy is 2–5%; recurrence may manifest as the same or a different NTD subtype.
2–3-fold increased NTD risk from periconceptional hyperglycaemia; tight glycaemic control before conception substantially reduces but does not eliminate risk.
Highest-risk anticonvulsant teratogen for NTDs; safer alternatives exist and should be considered before conception in women of childbearing age.
Reduces MTHFR enzyme activity and modestly raises NTD risk; high-dose folate supplementation compensates for much of this deficit.
Female fetuses are affected 2–4 times more often than male fetuses in most iniencephaly case series, a ratio that is even higher than for other NTD subtypes.

riskFactors.factor: Low socioeconomic status and food insecurity
riskFactors.description: Limited access to folate-rich foods and prenatal supplements correlates with higher NTD rates in all global regions without mandatory fortification.

affectedDemographics: Female fetuses at a disproportionately high rate (2–4 times higher than male fetuses)
Fetuses of all ethnicities; higher rates in populations without mandatory folic acid food fortification
Pregnancies in women with insulin-dependent diabetes
Fetuses exposed to valproate in the first four weeks of gestation
Families with a prior NTD-affected pregnancy and MTHFR gene variants
diagnosisOverview: Iniencephaly is almost always diagnosed prenatally. On first-trimester ultrasound at 11–14 weeks, the striking finding is an absent or very short neck with the fetal head in fixed retroflexion. The occipital bone defect and retroflexed spine are visible on both transabdominal and transvaginal scanning. Second-trimester anomaly scan at 18–22 weeks defines the full extent of the defect — including occipital bone loss, degree of retroflexion, vertebral anomalies, and any co-existing brain malformations such as anencephaly, encephalocele, or hydrocephalus. Maternal serum AFP is typically elevated, particularly in the apertus form. Amniocentesis measures AFP and acetylcholinesterase in amniotic fluid to confirm open NTD status, and chromosomal microarray or karyotype is offered. The key differential diagnosis is cervical spine hyperextension from other causes (cord entanglement, thyroid mass), which resolves with positional change, unlike the fixed retroflexion of iniencephaly. MRI may be offered to better characterise posterior fossa and brain anomalies when ultrasound views are limited.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies absent or very short neck, fixed retroflexion of the fetal head, and absent normal posterior cervical spine closure as the earliest signs of iniencephaly.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Transabdominal or transvaginal scan performed by an experienced sonographer; the fixed head retroflexion is visually distinctive and differentiates iniencephaly from positional hyperextension.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines the occipital bone defect, full extent of spinal dysraphism, degree of retroflexion, vertebral anomalies, and co-existing brain malformations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A comprehensive fetal anatomy survey with targeted assessment of the posterior fossa, cervical and thoracic spine, and brain structures; performed by a maternal-fetal medicine specialist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated in the apertus form of iniencephaly due to leakage of AFP from exposed neural tissue; supports the diagnosis and triggers further evaluation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw from the maternal arm between 15 and 20 weeks; results within a few days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms open NTD via elevated amniotic fluid AFP and positive acetylcholinesterase; provides fetal chromosomal material for karyotype or microarray.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fine needle under ultrasound guidance through the maternal abdomen; mild cramping expected; chromosomal results in 7–14 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes trisomy 18, trisomy 13, or other chromosomal anomalies present in a minority of cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on amniotic fluid; turnaround approximately 7–14 days for microarray.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides superior anatomical detail of posterior fossa, brainstem, and cervical spinal cord, especially when ultrasound views are suboptimal due to fetal position or maternal habitus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The mother lies in a wide-bore MRI scanner without radiation exposure; the scan takes approximately 30–45 minutes and is safe in the second and third trimesters.

treatmentOverview: Iniencephaly is universally lethal, and there is no corrective surgical or medical treatment. Management after prenatal diagnosis is focused entirely on the parents' needs and the family's wishes. Families are offered the full range of options: continuation of the pregnancy with perinatal palliative care, induction of labour at a gestational age agreed with the parents, or termination of pregnancy where legally available. When parents choose to continue, a multidisciplinary team — maternal-fetal medicine, neonatology, palliative care, psychology, and genetics — develops a detailed birth plan. Obstetric challenges are important: the fixed retroflexed fetal head and cervicothoracic spinal rigidity significantly increase the risk of obstructed labour and uterine rupture, so careful mode of delivery planning is essential; caesarean section is often required. Severe polyhydramnios, present in many cases, may require amnioreduction to relieve maternal discomfort and reduce preterm labour risk. Neonatal care after delivery is comfort-focused: warmth, gentle handling, and symptom management without resuscitation or ventilation. Genetic counselling before the next pregnancy is essential, and high-dose folic acid supplementation should begin at least three months before the next conception.

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose folic acid (prevention in subsequent pregnancy)
medicalTreatments.description: Women with a prior iniencephaly-affected pregnancy are advised to take 4–5 mg of folic acid daily beginning at least one month and ideally three months before the next conception, continuing through the first trimester.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces NTD recurrence risk by approximately 70% versus no supplementation (MRC Vitamin Study, Lancet 1991).

medicalTreatments.name: Amnioreduction
medicalTreatments.description: Serial ultrasound-guided removal of amniotic fluid for severe polyhydramnios causing maternal respiratory compromise, significant discomfort, or preterm labour risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides effective symptomatic relief in approximately 80–90% of procedures; does not alter fetal prognosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Perinatal palliative care programme
medicalTreatments.description: Structured interdisciplinary plan including delivery planning, comfort measures for the neonate, family support, and bereavement care, co-ordinated by neonatology, palliative care, and specialist midwifery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Consistently associated with better bereavement outcomes and parental satisfaction in perinatal hospice and palliative care programme evaluations.

medicalTreatments.name: Caesarean section for obstructed labour
medicalTreatments.description: Due to the fixed retroflexed fetal head and rigid cervicothoracic spine, normal vaginal delivery is often impossible or poses unacceptable uterine rupture risk; caesarean section is typically planned when the family continues the pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Eliminates risk of catastrophic uterine rupture or traumatic delivery complications; the fetal outcome is not improved but maternal safety is protected.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary folate optimisation
alternativeTreatments.description: Increasing intake of dark leafy vegetables, legumes, and fortified cereals complements folic acid supplementation, particularly in the periconceptional period.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pre-conception genetic counselling and MTHFR testing
alternativeTreatments.description: Identifying MTHFR 677C>T homozygosity guides dose selection for periconceptional supplementation and informs family recurrence counselling.

whySeeSpecialist: Referral to a maternal-fetal medicine specialist is essential immediately on suspicion of iniencephaly to confirm the diagnosis, map the full extent of the defect, and plan a safe delivery. A clinical geneticist should counsel the family on recurrence risk, MTHFR and chromosomal testing, and pre-pregnancy planning. Neonatology and palliative care teams are engaged before delivery to ensure a family-centred, dignified birth experience.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek care at a tertiary fetal medicine centre experienced in lethal fetal anomalies. Specifically ask whether the centre has an established perinatal palliative care pathway, a named bereavement support team, and experience in planning deliveries where obstructed labour is anticipated. Ask about post-mortem examination services for families who wish a thorough pathological diagnosis, which can refine recurrence risk.
preventionStrategies: Take 400 mcg of folic acid daily from at least one month before conception through the first 12 weeks; after a prior NTD, increase to 4–5 mg daily.
Eat folate-rich foods — leafy greens, legumes, fortified cereals — daily throughout the periconceptional period.
Achieve tight glycaemic control before conception if you have diabetes.
Discuss safer anticonvulsant alternatives with a neurologist if on valproate.
Support mandatory folic acid food fortification policies, the population-level intervention with the greatest proven impact on NTD rates.
lifestyleModifications: Begin a prenatal vitamin with 400–800 mcg of folic acid before stopping contraception.
Maintain a balanced folate-rich diet throughout the pre-pregnancy and first-trimester period.
Manage blood glucose carefully if you have diabetes.
Avoid alcohol, which impairs folate absorption.
Treat fever promptly in weeks 3–8 of early pregnancy to avoid hyperthermia-related risk.

recommended: Dark leafy greens — spinach, kale, broccoli — as the most folate-dense vegetables
Legumes — lentils, chickpeas, black beans — providing high folate per serving
Fortified cereals and breads providing 100% of the daily recommended folate intake
Citrus fruits and juices as an additional folate source
avoid: Excessive alcohol, which inhibits folate absorption and elevates homocysteine
Liver in large amounts during early pregnancy due to excess retinol risk
Unpasteurised dairy and raw meat for general food safety in pregnancy

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise before and during pregnancy is safe and recommended; it does not increase NTD risk. Avoid saunas, hot yoga, and hot tubs in weeks 3–8 of the first trimester because of hyperthermia risk to neural tube closure. Pregnant women carrying an iniencephaly-affected fetus should follow obstetric guidance regarding activity, with modifications for polyhydramnios if present.
dailyManagement: Engage with the perinatal palliative care team early to develop a birth plan before 30 weeks of gestation.
Report any signs of severe polyhydramnios promptly — rapid abdominal growth, breathlessness — for assessment.
Attend all scheduled fetal medicine and obstetric appointments; mode of delivery planning is critical.
Access psychological support and bereavement counselling early; waiting times for specialist bereavement services can be lengthy.
Before the next pregnancy, complete genetic counselling, confirm folic acid dose, and discuss pre-conception optimisation with the MFM team.
prognosis: Iniencephaly is universally lethal. Most affected fetuses are stillborn. Of the very rare liveborn infants, survival beyond a few hours has not been consistently documented in the published literature; a handful of case reports describe survival of days, usually in the clausus (skin-covered) variant, but all such cases died rapidly. No liveborn infant with iniencephaly has survived with meaningful neurological function. For families, recurrence risk is 2–5% for any NTD in a subsequent pregnancy, reducible by approximately 70% with high-dose folic acid supplementation. Psychological outcomes for parents are meaningfully improved by access to specialist perinatal palliative care, bereavement support, and genetic counselling.
recoveryTimeline: There is no recovery timeline for the infant. For parents, acute grief is typically most intense in the weeks and months following the loss. Adaptation is gradual over 1–2 years; anniversaries and subsequent pregnancies may trigger renewed grief. A proportion of parents experience prolonged grief disorder requiring professional intervention. Planning a subsequent pregnancy is physiologically possible after one menstrual cycle, but emotionally many families benefit from waiting 6–12 months.
complications: Obstructed labour — the fixed retroflexed head and rigid spine create a mechanical obstruction that makes vaginal delivery impossible or highly dangerous in many cases, with risk of uterine rupture.
Polyhydramnios — excess amniotic fluid occurs in a significant proportion, causing maternal discomfort, respiratory compromise, and preterm labour risk.
Prolonged pregnancy — absent or severely impaired hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis function delays onset of labour.
Maternal psychological morbidity — depression, anxiety, and PTSD occur in 20–30% of mothers following perinatal loss from a lethal fetal anomaly.
Recurrence risk for NTD — without high-dose periconceptional folic acid, risk is 2–5% in a subsequent pregnancy.

supportResources.name: NHS — Neural Tube Defects
supportResources.description: NHS guidance on lethal NTDs including anencephaly and related conditions, with management options and support.

supportResources.name: CDC — Folic Acid and Neural Tube Defects
supportResources.description: CDC guidance on folic acid supplementation for NTD prevention, including dose recommendations for women with prior affected pregnancies.

supportResources.name: NICHD — Neural Tube Defects Information
supportResources.description: NICHD patient information on NTD causes, risk factors, and prevention.

sources.title: Dhall U et al. Iniencephaly: a review. International Journal of Morphology, 2011

confusedWithConditions.name: Anencephaly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anencephaly involves only the absent skull and brain, without the fixed retroflexion of the spine and occipital bone defect that define iniencephaly; iniencephaly can coexist with anencephaly.

confusedWithConditions.name: Craniorachischisis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Craniorachischisis involves the entire spinal neural tube being open from head to sacrum, without the specific retroflexion and occipital bone defect of iniencephaly; both are lethal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Occipital encephalocele
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Occipital encephalocele is herniation of brain tissue through a posterior skull defect, with an intact calvarium and no fixed retroflexion; it is compatible with survival in many cases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Arnold-Chiari syndrome with spina bifida
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chiari II malformation with spina bifida involves hindbrain herniation and spinal dysraphism, but the head is not fixed in retroflexion and the skull is largely intact; many cases are survivable.

relatedConditions.name: Anencephaly
relatedConditions.relevance: Iniencephaly may coexist with anencephaly and shares the same neural tube closure failure mechanism, folate deficiency risk factor, and lethal prognosis.

relatedConditions.name: Craniorachischisis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most severe NTD, involving total neural tube failure; iniencephaly represents a less extensive but equally lethal variant of the same closure failure spectrum.

relatedConditions.name: Occipital encephalocele
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the posterior cranial location of the defect; encephalocele involves a focal herniation rather than retroflexion, and is often survivable.

relatedConditions.name: Spina bifida
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common NTD; shares the same folate deficiency risk factor and prevention strategy as iniencephaly.

relatedConditions.name: Microcephaly
relatedConditions.relevance: Another major congenital brain structural defect that is detected prenatally and requires specialist fetal medicine assessment.

faqs.question: What is iniencephaly?
faqs.answer: Iniencephaly is a rare, fatal neural tube defect in which the occipital bone is defective, the head is permanently bent backward onto the spine (retroflexion), and the cervical or cervicothoracic spine is open. The name comes from the Greek word for occiput. It is always lethal.

faqs.question: Is iniencephaly always fatal?
faqs.answer: Yes. Iniencephaly is universally lethal. Most affected fetuses are stillborn. The rare infants born alive survive only minutes to hours. No liveborn infant with iniencephaly has survived with meaningful neurological function.

faqs.question: What causes iniencephaly?
faqs.answer: Iniencephaly results from failure of the posterior neural tube to close properly at the occiput and upper cervical spine during weeks 3–4 of pregnancy, combined with a primary defect in occipital bone formation. Key risk factors include folate deficiency, maternal diabetes, valproate use, and genetic variants in folate-pathway and neural closure genes.

faqs.question: How is iniencephaly diagnosed?
faqs.answer: It is diagnosed prenatally on ultrasound. From 11 weeks, the fixed retroflexed head and absent neck are visible. The second-trimester anomaly scan at 18–22 weeks maps the full defect. Elevated maternal serum AFP and amniocentesis with acetylcholinesterase confirm open NTD status. Chromosomal microarray excludes co-existing chromosomal anomalies.

faqs.question: How is iniencephaly different from anencephaly?
faqs.answer: Anencephaly involves absence of the skull and cerebral hemispheres without fixed retroflexion of the spine. Iniencephaly additionally features a defective occipital bone, the head locked backward against the back, and an open cervical spine. They are related NTD subtypes and can coexist.

faqs.question: Can iniencephaly be prevented?
faqs.answer: Risk is substantially reduced by taking 400 mcg of folic acid daily from at least one month before conception. Women with a prior affected pregnancy should take 4–5 mg daily. Avoiding valproate, controlling diabetes before conception, and avoiding fever in the first 8 weeks of pregnancy are also recommended preventive measures.

faqs.question: What is the difference between iniencephaly and spina bifida?
faqs.answer: Spina bifida is a focal spinal NTD with an intact skull and variable neurological function — many children with spina bifida survive and lead active lives. Iniencephaly is a lethal defect combining an occipital bone defect, fixed head retroflexion, and open cervical spine; no child with iniencephaly has survived.

faqs.question: Why is the head bent backward in iniencephaly?
faqs.answer: The fixed backward bending of the head (retroflexion) occurs because the occipital bone defect leaves a gap at the skull base, and the open, malformed cervical spine cannot support normal head posture. The rigid retroflexed angle is fixed in utero and cannot be corrected.

faqs.question: What delivery method is used for iniencephaly pregnancies?
faqs.answer: The fixed retroflexed head and rigid spine make vaginal delivery mechanically difficult or impossible and carry a risk of uterine rupture. Caesarean section is often required. Obstetric planning in a specialist centre is essential before the expected delivery date when the family continues the pregnancy.

faqs.question: How rare is iniencephaly?
faqs.answer: Iniencephaly is estimated to occur in fewer than 1 per 100,000 pregnancies. It is much rarer than anencephaly or spina bifida, with fewer than several hundred cases reported in the world medical literature.

faqs.question: What is iniencephaly apertus?
faqs.answer: In iniencephaly apertus (the open form), the spinal defect is not covered by skin, so the spinal cord is directly exposed to amniotic fluid. This elevates amniotic AFP and acetylcholinesterase more dramatically. It is the more common form and is slightly easier to diagnose prenatally.

faqs.question: What is iniencephaly clausus?
faqs.answer: In iniencephaly clausus (the closed form), skin covers the spinal defect, making it a closed NTD. AFP elevation in maternal serum may be less marked. The occipital bone defect and retroflexion are still present. Both forms are lethal.

faqs.question: What brain abnormalities occur with iniencephaly?
faqs.answer: Coexisting brain abnormalities are found in the majority of cases and include anencephaly in some combined forms, occipital encephalocele, hydrocephalus, Dandy-Walker malformation, and holoprosencephaly. These are identified on the detailed second-trimester anatomy scan and fetal MRI.

faqs.question: What is the recurrence risk for iniencephaly?
faqs.answer: As with all NTDs, after one affected pregnancy the recurrence risk for any NTD is approximately 2–5%. Taking 4–5 mg of folic acid daily from at least one month before the next conception reduces this risk by roughly 70%. Genetic counselling is recommended.

faqs.question: Is iniencephaly associated with chromosomal abnormalities?
faqs.answer: A minority of iniencephaly cases co-exist with chromosomal abnormalities, particularly trisomy 18 or trisomy 13. Chromosomal microarray or karyotype is offered to all families via amniocentesis to exclude these co-existing conditions.

faqs.question: Why are female fetuses more often affected by iniencephaly?
faqs.answer: Female fetuses are affected 2–4 times more often than male fetuses in most case series, a ratio higher than seen in other NTD subtypes. The biological explanation is not established; it may relate to sex differences in neural tube closure timing, folate metabolism, or selective intrauterine survival of affected fetuses.

faqs.question: What support is available after an iniencephaly diagnosis?
faqs.answer: Specialist perinatal palliative care, bereavement midwifery, genetic counselling, and psychological support are available at tertiary fetal medicine centres. Peer support organisations for families affected by NTDs and lethal fetal anomalies exist in many countries. Accessing support early — before delivery — is associated with better bereavement outcomes.

faqs.question: Does iniencephaly cause polyhydramnios?
faqs.answer: Yes, polyhydramnios — too much amniotic fluid — is a common complication, occurring because the fixed retroflexed posture and co-existing brain anomalies impair the fetal swallowing that normally regulates amniotic fluid volume. Severe polyhydramnios can cause maternal breathlessness, abdominal discomfort, and risk of preterm labour.

faqs.question: Can iniencephaly be treated with surgery in utero?
faqs.answer: No. Unlike certain forms of spina bifida, where fetal surgery has shown modest benefit, iniencephaly cannot be corrected surgically. The structural defects — absent occipital bone, fixed retroflexion, and open spine with degenerated neural tissue — are not amenable to repair. Management is entirely palliative.

metaDescription: Iniencephaly is a lethal NTD where the skull base, neck, and upper spine fail to form normally, locking the head backward. Learn causes, diagnosis, and prevention.
